Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to dedicate about 25 hours a week, but with limited resources at home, I had to be creative about when and where I could actually focus. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time drawing diagrams and creating my own charts from the material, but that happened mostly away from the computer. I'd batch my studying into longer sessions when I could get access to a quiet space.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I couldn't always count on having consistent internet access or a quiet place to study, so I had to grab opportunities when they came. Sometimes I'd have a productive week, other times life just got in the way. Deadlines would push me to find a way to get things done, but regular scheduling was really hard for me to maintain.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check, like if a peer messaged me or if I remembered there was a deadline coming. Sometimes my study group friends would remind me about something posted in the discussions. I didn't log in just to browse around—it felt more purposeful when I did go on, even if it wasn't that frequent.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me because my internet access isn't always reliable. When I did get online, I tried to make the most of it—downloading what I could, checking messages, participating in discussions. But it never felt automatic or routine the way I imagine it does for students who are always connected.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually really valuable to me. I posted when I could and interacted with my peers quite a bit—those 29 interactions meant a lot because I learned so much from hearing other perspectives. The visual materials like diagrams and slides were helpful too since that matches how I learn best. Peer support really carried me through some tough weeks.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos because they required too much bandwidth and time online. I'd try to watch them but could only get through about half an hour's worth before connectivity issues or time constraints stopped me. Some of the text-heavy readings I'd skim rather than deeply engage with because they weren't visual enough for me to really absorb.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to the bigger picture or when it involved working with other students. The peer discussions were great because I felt like I was contributing and learning simultaneously. If something was just busywork or didn't seem to lead anywhere concrete, I'd struggle to prioritize it over other demands in my life.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. When you have limited time and resources, you have to triage, and required work naturally comes first. But sometimes the optional stuff—like extra discussion threads—was actually more engaging because there was less pressure. I just couldn't always get to them.
Q9: Absolutely, this was huge for me. I'd download PDFs and slides whenever I could get online, then study them offline for hours. I made handwritten notes, drew visual maps of concepts, and worked through problems on paper. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed materials or downloaded files, completely invisible to the system.
Q10: Most of my studying looked like me sitting with printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams at my kitchen table or at a friend's place. I'd work through concepts by sketching them out visually, testing myself with flashcards I made. None of that showed up anywhere online. My real engagement was happening offline, in my notebooks and in conversations with peers outside the platform.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture slide or reading once, download it immediately, and then spend days working through it offline. My one login might have looked like I barely glanced at something, but I was actually studying that material intensively for hours afterward. It was the only practical way for me to learn given my situation.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when my anxiety would spike. Even though my stress was generally low, the moderate anxiety I carry made it hard to focus sometimes. When assignments piled up and I fell behind on completion, it became this cycle where I felt worse about engaging. My low resilience meant setbacks hit me hard.
Q13: Definitely. Having low resources meant I was often juggling other responsibilities—work, helping family, managing limited internet time. Fatigue was real because I was putting in the study hours but not always in ideal conditions. Sometimes I'd log into the LMS exhausted and just not have the mental energy to engage meaningfully with what was there.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd be on the LMS but my mind was elsewhere. I'd scroll through materials or open videos but not really absorb anything because I was tired or anxious about other things. It's frustrating because the activity log would show I was there, but I wasn't really present. Those sessions felt wasted.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was anxiety—starting felt overwhelming when I wasn't sure I could do well. Part of it was practical, waiting until I had proper time and space to focus. My completion rate suffered because of this pattern, and I regret not pushing through sooner.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of when I'd really engage with the LMS. I'd ramp up my logins and activity as due dates approached. Without that external pressure, it was too easy to let the platform slip down my priority list. The structure helped, but it also meant my engagement was very uneven throughout the semester.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially from peers, it motivated me to stay involved. The peer support intervention really helped my mood and made me feel less alone in struggling. But feedback on assignments came slowly sometimes, and by then I'd already moved on mentally. Timely encouragement made a real difference when it happened.
Q18: The course structure was challenging for someone in my situation. It seemed designed for students who could engage consistently online, which wasn't realistic for me. The peer interaction components were great and played to my strengths, but the overall pacing assumed a level of constant access I couldn't maintain. I fell through the cracks.
Q19: The discussion forums and peer support elements made engagement easier—I thrived in those spaces. But the heavy reliance on video content and frequent online check-ins made things harder given my connectivity issues. If there had been more downloadable, visual materials from the start, I could have engaged more effectively on my own terms.
Q20: I can relate to that pattern somewhat. You might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or genuinely engaging with ideas but struggling to translate that into completed submissions. For me, the gap between understanding material and actually finishing assignments was about anxiety and perfectionism. Sometimes you're working hard but the output doesn't reflect it.
Q21: That actually sounds like my experience in reverse—I had decent engagement in some ways but my exam score and final grade didn't show it. I think students can learn effectively through methods that don't register online. If someone has strong study habits offline or learns through peer conversations, their LMS activity might look minimal while their understanding is solid.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, which captures some patterns. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of your attention, emotional engagement, and learning that happens through peer conversations outside the platform. For visual learners like me who work with printed materials, the logs show almost nothing of real value.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and feeling connected to the learning community. It's not just logging in—it's thinking about concepts when you're away from the screen, discussing ideas with peers, and genuinely trying to grow. My peer interactions and the happiness I felt from that support—that was real engagement, even if my grades don't show it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with hand-drawn diagrams and printed notes, or the meaningful conversations I had with classmates outside the platform. It wouldn't capture that peer support genuinely lifted my mood and kept me going when I wanted to give up. My engagement was real and often deep—it just happened in spaces the system couldn't see.
